elements.sent-2411-001: We develop an excursion theory that describes the evolution of a Markov process indexed by a Lévy tree away from a regular and instantaneous point $x$ of the state space.
elements.sent-2411-002: The theory builds upon a notion of local time at $x$ that was recently introduced in \cite{2024structure .}
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite the radically different setting, our results exhibit striking similarities to the classical excursion theory for $\mathbb{R _+$-indexed Markov processes.}
elements.sent-2411-004: We then show that the genealogy of the excursions can be encoded in a Lévy tree called the tree coded by the local time.
elements.sent-2411-005: In particular, we recover by different methods the excursion theory of Abraham and Le Gall \cite{ALG15 , which was developed for Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The purpose of this work is to develop an excursion theory for Markov processes indexed by Lévy trees, away from a regular and instantaneous point $x$ of the state space.
elements.sent-2411-007: This theory builds upon a notion of local time process introduced recently in the companion paper \cite{2024structure .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Informally, a Markov process indexed by a rooted $\mathbb{R}$-tree can be understood as follows: the Markov process starts at the root of the tree at a point in the state space, travels along the branches of the tree away from the root, and at every branching point it splits into independent copies that continue to evolve with the same dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this work, the tree itself is random, and to ensure that the tree-indexed process possesses basic Markovian properties the family of random trees that we consider are Lévy trees.
elements.sent-2411-010: This class of random rooted $\mathbb{R}$-trees plays a central role in modern probability theory.
elements.sent-2411-011: For instance, they appear as scaling limits of Galton-Watson trees \cite{DLG02 and, as their discrete analogs, they are characterised by a branching property \cite{WeillLevyTrees .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: One notable member of this family is the celebrated Brownian tree\footnote{Also known as Aldous' Continuum Random Tree \cite{AdousCRT with free total mass.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: We refer to \cite{AdousCRT, AldousTreeBasedModels, Btree and references within for background and related works.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The concept of a Markov process indexed by a Lévy tree has appeared over the years in various settings, making it also an important canonical probabilistic object.
elements.sent-2411-015: They are closely related to the theory of super-processes \cite{DLG02 , and Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree has been essential for the study of the so-called Brownian geometry -- a family of random surfaces arising as scaling limits of random planar maps \cite{baur2016classification, bettinelli2022compact, CLGmodif, LG11,Mie11 .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: We refer to the discussion at the end of this introduction for a more detailed account.
elements.sent-2411-017: We shall see that, despite the radically different setting, this theory shares striking similarities with classical excursion theory for $\mathbb{R _+$-indexed Markov processes.}
elements.sent-2411-018: To highlight these similarities, let us start by briefly recalling some elements of the classical theory.
elements.sent-2411-019: For the purposes of this work, we further assume that the motion is continuous, the point $x$ is recurrent and $\int_0^\infty \mathrm{d s~\mathbbm{1 _{\xi_s=x =0$ a.s.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The path of the Markov process can be decomposed in excursions away from $x$, where if we write $(a_i,b_i)_{i \in \mathcal{I }$ for the connected components of $\mathbb{R _+ \setminus \{ t \geq 0 : \xi_t = x \}$, the excursion associated with the excursion interval $(a_i,b_i)$ is the piece of path given by $\xi^{i,* := (\xi_{(a_i + t)\wedge b_i : t \geq 0)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The study of the family of excursions relies on a remarkable continuous additive functional $(\mathcal{L _t)_{t \geq 0}$ of the Markov process named the local time at $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: This process is unique up to a multiplicative constant that we fix arbitrarily.
elements.sent-2411-023: Due to the fact that the support of the Stieltjes measure $\dd \mathcal{L}$ is precisely the set $\{t \geq 0 : \xi_t = x \}$, the local time is well suited to index the family of excursions, and the order induced by $(\mathcal{L _t)_{t \geq 0}$ is consistent with the order induced by time.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The main result of the theory states that the point measure \begin{equation}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}} \delta_{(\mathcal{L}_{a_i}, \xi^{i,*})}\end{equation} is a Poisson point measure with intensity $\mathbbm{1 _{\mathbb{R _+ (t)\dd t \otimes \mathcal{N}$, where $\mathcal{N}$ is an infinite measure called the excursion measure.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Informally, one can think of $\mathcal{N}$ as describing the law of a typical excursion.
elements.sent-2411-026: For historical reasons, the point measure in the last display is often called the excursion process of $(\xi_t)_{t \geq 0}$.
elements.sent-2411-027: In addition to the collection of excursions, there is another closely related family of trajectories that plays an important role in the theory.
elements.sent-2411-028: Namely, it is natural to consider the family of trajectories obtained by shifting $(\xi, \mathcal{L )$ at the starting point of an excursion, i.e. $(\xi^i,\mathcal{L ^i):=((\xi_{a_i+t ,\mathcal{L _{a_i+t ):~t\geq 0)$, $i\in \mathcal{I}$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-033: Lévy trees are constructed from excursions above the running infimum of a subclass of spectrally positive Lévy processes, and their law is determined by the corresponding Laplace exponent $\psi$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Any Lévy tree is equipped with a clockwise exploration that informally, starts at the root of the tree and travels along the branches from ``left to right", following the contour of the tree.
elements.sent-2411-035: From the corresponding $\psi$-Lévy excursion, one can construct a functional $H= (H_t)_{t \geq 0}$ called the height function, which encodes at every $t\geq 0$ the distance to the root during the clockwise exploration.
elements.sent-2411-036: The tree and its clockwise exploration can be fully recovered from $H$, which is why we denote the corresponding tree by $\mathcal{T _H$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: These constructions hold under the excursion measure above the running infimum of the $\psi$-Lévy process, as well as under the underlying probability measure.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the former case, the tree $\mathcal{T _H$ is compact and referred to as a $\psi$-Lévy tree.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In the latter, the resulting tree is no longer compact, but it can be thought as a concatenation of $\psi$-Lévy trees at their root - one for each excursion of the Lévy process above its ruining infimum.
elements.sent-2411-040: For this reason, $\mathcal{T _H$ under the underlying probability measure is referred to as a forest of $\psi$-Lévy trees.}
elements.sent-2411-041: For each point $a \in \mathcal{T _H$, we write $(\xi_a, \mathcal{L _a)$ for the value at the point $a$ of the Markov process paired with its local time at $x$.}}

elements.sent-2411-049: The interiors of excursion components play a role analogous to the one of excursion intervals in the time-indexed setting, but the former are of a more intricate nature due to the fact that each $\mathcal{C _u$ is itself a random tree.}

elements.sent-2411-051: The family of excursions $(\xi^{u,* )_{u \in \mathcal{D }$ can be indexed by means of a remarkable additive functional $(A_t)_{t \geq 0}$, recently introduced in \cite{2024structure , which plays a crucial role in this work.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Informally, at each time $t \geq 0$, the variable $A_t$ measures the amount of time spent by the clockwise exploration of the tree at points with label $x$ up to time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Hence, the process $(A_t)_{t \geq 0}$ plays the role of the local time at $x$ for the tree-indexed process when the tree is explored in clockwise order.

elements.sent-2411-192: We conclude this introduction with a discussion concerning the relation of our theory with the closely related work of Abraham and Le Gall \cite{ALG15 , as well as with some connections to related theories and open problems.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Connection with Abraham-Le Gall excursion theory \cite{ALG15 .}
elements.sent-2411-194: An excursion theory for Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree was developed by Abraham and Le Gall in \cite{ALG15 using radically different methods.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Specifically, \cite{ALG15 considers the case when $(\xi_t)_{t \geq 0}$ is a linear Brownian motion, the underlying tree is a Brownian tree, and $x=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-196: The work \cite{ALG15 relies on symmetries and scaling properties of Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree, and these arguments cannot be exploited in our general setting.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Another key difference with the present work is that \cite{ALG15 does not introduce a notion of local time suitable to index the excursions.}
elements.sent-2411-198: In the last section of this work, we verify the consistency of our excursion theory with that in \cite{ALG15 ; that is, we show that the excursion measure introduced in \cite{ALG15 and their notion of boundary measure coincide with those in this work in the special case of Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: In the process, we derive the master formula \cite[Theorem 23]{ALG15 as a particular case of the exit formula [Theorem \ref{theorem:exit ].}}
elements.sent-2411-200: Finally, Corollary \ref{corolary:condlaw is a more precise version of \cite[Theorem 40]{ALG15 .}}
elements.sent-2411-201: We stress that although the methods employed in \cite{ALG15 are drastically different from those used here, the results and ideas employed by Abraham and Le Gall have been fundamental for the development of this manuscript.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Furthermore some results in \cite{ALG15 are exclusive to Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree and therefore can not be established in our general setting.}
elements.sent-2411-204: As in \cite{ALG15 , one of the main motivations of this work is the connection between Lévy trees with labels and continuous models of 2D random geometry.}
elements.sent-2411-205: As mentioned above, Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree has served as building block for the family of random metric spaces that are generically referred to as Brownian geometries \cite{LeGallLectures, LeGallRiera-nonCompactModels .}
elements.sent-2411-206: One notable member of this rich family is the celebrated Brownian sphere (or Brownian map) \cite{LG11, Mie11 .}
elements.sent-2411-207: The theory developed in \cite{ALG15 has been shown to be a powerful tool in the study of Brownian geometries, and specially when establishing spatial Markov properties -- continuous analogues of peelings of planar maps \cite{peeling .}}
elements.sent-2411-208: In short, when performing a metric exploration in a Brownian surface (by considering, for example, increasing balls or hulls) the space is divided progressively in discovered and undiscovered components.
elements.sent-2411-209: Spatial Markov properties characterise the distribution of these components conditional on their boundary lengths.
elements.sent-2411-210: The present excursion theory should allow to obtain more precise versions of these results, and should permit for instance to recover the initial surface through a gluing procedure of the components.
elements.sent-2411-211: As a notable example, the Brownian sphere can be divided in the Lévy net \cite{Axiomatic, Subor (coded by the tree of local time) and a collection of disjoint Brownian disks (which are coded by excursions of Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree), that have been shown to be independent conditionally on their boundary lengths \cite{fragmentations .}}
elements.sent-2411-212: Our work should allow for the reconstruction of the Brownian sphere by gluing the Brownian disks back into the Lévy net, in a completely explicit way.
elements.sent-2411-213: In recent years, new models of 2D random geometries closely related to Brownian motion indexed by stable trees have been introduced, see e.g. \cite{archer2024stable,kortchemski2024random .}
elements.sent-2411-214: Our results should shed light in the study of these new models and permit to solve questions that until now where out of reach.
elements.sent-2411-215: It is expected that these geometries can be connected to different versions of Lévy nets \cite{Axiomatic and to stable causal maps \cite{causal:maps .}}
elements.sent-2411-216: In particular, we hope to obtain similar reconstruction results for these models, and this is currently a work in progress.
elements.sent-2411-217: We also expect the present theory to relate Brownian motion indexed by stable trees to large classes of growth-fragmentation processes, and more precisely with the recently introduced family of self-similar Markov trees \cite{BCR:2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-218: These conform a family of decorated $\mathbb{R}$-trees that code the genealogy of general growth-fragmentation processes, and are scaling limits of multi-type Galton–Watson trees when the types along branches are in the domain of attraction of a positive self-similar Markov process.
elements.sent-2411-219: This connection has already been observed in the special case of Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree \cite{fragmentations thanks to \cite{ALG15 , see also Section~4.3 in \cite{BCR:2024 , and the stable case seems to be connected to some discret models of parking on trees \cite{BCR:2024,C:C:Parking,chen2021enumerationfullyparkedtrees .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-220: Another direction that has not been yet explored are invariance principles towards the excursion measure $\mathbf{N ^*_x$.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Invariance principles for Lévy snakes are an important topic \cite{Marzouk , and we hope that the definition of the excursion measure $\mathbf{N _x^*$ in terms of a pruning operation of $\mathbf{N _{x,0}$ will be useful to extend known convergence results.}}}
elements.sent-2411-222: Finally, it has been recently pointed out to us that the additive $A$ should also appear in scaling limit results concerning local times of Branching random walks and this direction is currently under study \cite{Bai-Chen-Hu .}
elements.sent-2411-223: The rest of the work is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-224: In Section \ref{section:preliminaries we recall the main elements of the theory of Lévy trees and Lévy snakes that are needed for this work.}
elements.sent-2411-225: In Section \ref{section:framework we fix the setup and hypotheses under which we develop the excursion theory, these are maintained throughout the rest of the manuscript.}
elements.sent-2411-226: In Section \ref{section:excursionstrajectories we introduce multiple functionals and notions that will be used extensively -- namely, the truncation operation on snake paths, the exit local time $(L_t)_{t \geq 0}$, debut points, the notion of excursion away from $x$ and the one of a subtrajectory stemming from a debut point.}

elements.sent-2411-229: The main aspects of the excursion theory are developed in Section \ref{section:excursiontheory .}

elements.sent-2411-232: These constitute the main results of this work.
elements.sent-2411-233: In Section \ref{section:L we develop a theory, analogous to that of exit local times, under the measures $\mathbf{N _{x,r , \mathbf{N _{x ^*$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: For instance, we prove a version of the special Markov property [Proposition \ref{proposition:L* ] under $\mathbf{N _{x,r}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-235: Section \ref{section:joinlaw is devoted to the study of the relation between the so-called tree coded by the local time and the family of excursions.}

elements.sent-2411-241: This section is divided into two main parts.
elements.sent-2411-242: We begin Section \ref{section:LablesandTrees by introducing a general construction of trees that is well suited for defining (possibly random) labels on them.}
elements.sent-2411-243: This construction will allow us to define the notion of a Markov process indexed by a tree at the end of Section \ref{section:LablesandTrees .}
elements.sent-2411-244: We then proceed in Section \ref{section:MPonLT to randomise the underlying tree within the class of Lévy trees.}
elements.sent-2411-245: This is done by making use of the classical theory of Lévy trees and Lévy snakes \cite{DLG02 , and for ease of reading we provide a brief overview of both theories.}
elements.sent-2411-246: If $M$ is a Polish space, we will use the standard notation $\mathbb{C (\mathbb{R _+, M)$ and $\mathbb{D (\mathbb{R _+, M)$ respectively for the space of $M$-valued continuous and rcll\footnote{Right-continuous with left limits.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: These spaces are endowed with the topology of local uniform convergence and the Skorokhod topology respectively, along with the corresponding Borel sigma-fields.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Labels on trees coded by excursions and snakes
elements.sent-2411-248: The family of trees that we work with are coded by continuous non-negative functions on $\mathbb{R _+$, that we broadly refer to as coding functions.}
elements.sent-2411-249: The construction is classic, and we recall its main elements in Section~\ref{sec:trees:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-250: Similarly, the labels on the tree are also defined in terms of a path-valued function in $\mathbb{R _+$ that is generically referred to as a snake trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-251: This notion is introduced and studied in Section \ref{secsnake .}
elements.sent-2411-252: In this short section, we recall standard notation and basic properties of $\mathbb{R}$-trees.
elements.sent-2411-253: For a detailed account, the reader is referred to the lecture notes by Evans \cite{evans or by Le Gall \cite{LG05 .}}
elements.sent-2411-254: An $\mathbb{R}$-tree $(\mathcal{T ,d)$ is a uniquely arcwise connected metric space, in which each arc is isometric to a compact interval of $\mathbb{R}$.}
elements.sent-2411-255: In this work, we exclusively consider rooted $\mathbb{R}$-trees, which further imposes $\mathcal{T}$ to have a distinguished point $\vartheta \in \mathcal{T}$, called the root.
elements.sent-2411-256: For every $u,v\in \mathcal{T}$ we write $\llbracket u,v \rrbracket$ for the range of the unique injective path connecting $u$ and $v$, and $u \curlywedge v$ for the unique element of $\mathcal{T}$ verifying the relation $\llbracket \vartheta , u \curlywedge v \rrbracket = \llbracket \vartheta , u \rrbracket \cap \llbracket \vartheta , v \rrbracket$.
elements.sent-2411-257: It is natural to define a partial order $\preceq$ coding the genealogy of $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-258: Namely, we shall write $u \preceq v$ if $u\in\llbracket \vartheta , v \rrbracket$, and when the latter holds we say that $u$ is an ancestor of $v$.
elements.sent-2411-259: The element $u \curlywedge v$ is called the common ancestor of $u$ and $v$.
elements.sent-2411-260: The multiplicity of $u$ is defined as the (possibly infinite) number of connected components of $\mathcal{T \setminus \{ u \}$.}
elements.sent-2411-261: For every $i \in \mathbb{N ^* \cup { \infty }$, we write $\text{Mult _i(\mathcal{T )$ for the family of points in $\mathcal{T}$ with multiplicity $i$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-262: These definitions allow us to extend classic notions from discrete trees to the framework of $\mathbb{R}$-trees.
elements.sent-2411-263: For instance, we shall make use of the following standard nomenclature:.
elements.sent-2411-264: The elements of $\text{Mult _1(\mathcal{T )$ are the leaves of $\mathcal{T}$;.}}
elements.sent-2411-265: The elements with multiplicity at least $2$ conform the skeleton of $\mathcal{T}$;.
elements.sent-2411-266: The elements with multiplicity at least $3$ are referred to as the branching points of $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-267: Let us now present a canonical way to construct $\mathbb{R}$-trees in terms of continuous non-negative functions.
elements.sent-2411-268: This method is standard and we refer to \cite{Duq.trees.notes for a thorough study on coding of $\mathbb{R}$-trees.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Fix a continuous non-negative function $h: \mathbb{R _+ \mapsto \mathbb{R _+$ and set \begin{equation}\label{def:sigma:h} \sigma_h:=\inf \big\{t\geq 0:~h(s)=0 \, \text{ for every }s \geq t\big\}\end{equation} for its lifetime, where as usual $\inf \emptyset = \infty$ (this convention will be maintained throughout this work).}}
elements.sent-2411-270: For every $s, t \in \mathbb{R _+$ we set \begin{equation}\label{def:m:h} \displaystyle m_{h}(s,t):= \inf_{s\wedge t\leq r \leq s\vee t}h(r),\end{equation} and, with the convention $[a,\infty]:=[a,\infty)$ for $a\in \mathbb{R}$, we define a pseudometric $d_h$ on $[0,\sigma_h]$ by \begin{equation}\label{def:d:h} d_h(s,t) := h(s) + h(t) - 2 \cdot m_{h}(s , t), \quad \text{for }s,t \in [0,\sigma_h].\end{equation}.}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Markov processes indexed by Lévy trees
elements.sent-2411-338: The objective of the section is to introduce the notion of a Markov process indexed by a $\psi$-Lévy tree.
elements.sent-2411-339: To this end, we first present $\psi$-Lévy trees through their coding function, the so-called height process of a $\psi$-Lévy process.
elements.sent-2411-340: We will then assign labels to the $\psi$-Lévy tree by considering the snake driven by the height process of the Lévy tree.
elements.sent-2411-341: The law of the height process is determined by a function $\psi:\mathbb{R _+\to \mathbb{R _+$ known as its branching mechanism.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: In this work, we always assume that $\psi$ can be written in the following form \begin{equation}\label{equation:psi} \psi(\lambda)=\alpha\lambda+\beta \lambda^{2}+\int_{(0,\infty)}\pi(\dd x)\big(\exp(-\lambda x)-1+\lambda x\big), \quad \lambda \geq 0,\end{equation} for some non-negative $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R _+$, and where $\pi(\dd x)$ is a measure on $(0,\infty)$ satisfying the integrability condition $\int_{(0,\infty) \pi(\dd x) (x\wedge x^{2 )<\infty$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-343: We impose also the following additional condition on the branching mechanism \begin{equation}\label{1_infinity_psi} \int_{1}^{\infty}\frac{\dd \lambda}{\psi(\lambda)}<\infty,\end{equation} which in particular will ensure that the corresponding Lévy tree is compact.
elements.sent-2411-344: It is classic that for every such function $\psi$ one can associate a spectrally positive Lévy process with Laplace exponent $\psi$.

elements.section-2411-009: The excursion process and the tree coded by the local time
elements.sent-2411-1077: The goal of this section is to prove that the genealogy of the excursions as well as their respective boundary sizes can be encoded in a random real tree.
elements.sent-2411-1078: In the process, our study will shed light on the law of the excursion process $\mathcal{E}$ under $\mathbb{N _{x,0}$, thereby complementing the statement of Theorem \ref{theorem:excursionPPP which solely concerned the law of $\mathcal{E}$ under $\mathbb{P _{0,x,0}$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-1080: By convention, $\widetilde{H (\upvarrho , \overline{\omega )$ is set as the path identically equal to $\widehat{\text{\lambdaC _0$ if the convergence in \eqref{equation:aproximacionA:def does not hold\footnote{When working under measures associated to Lévy snakes, this will not occur outside of a negligible set, but we give a general definition in order to have a comprehensible framework.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1081: As usual, when there is no risk of confusion we will drop the dependence on $(\upvarrho , \overline{\omega )$.}
elements.sent-2411-1082: The process $ \widetilde{H}$ was studied under $\mathbb{P _{0,x,0}$ and $\mathbb{N _{x,0}$ in \cite{2024structure and for latter use we recall some of its main properties.}}}

elements.sent-2411-1084: Hence, under $\mathbb{P _{0,x,0}$ and $\mathbb{N _{x,0}$, the real tree $\mathcal{T _{\widetilde{H }$ is well-defined, and it is distributed as a forest of $\widetilde{\psi}$-Lévy trees and as a $\widetilde{\psi}$-Lévy tree respectively, in the sense of Section \ref{subsection:height .}}}}}

elements.section-2411-010: Consistency with Abraham-Le Gall's excursion theory
elements.sent-2411-1184: We conclude this work verifying the consistency of our excursion theory with the one developed by Abraham and Le Gall \cite{ALG15 for Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree.}
elements.sent-2411-1185: In this section, we assume that $\psi(\lambda) := \lambda^2/2$ for $\lambda \in \mathbb{R _+$, that the Polish space $(E,\dd _E) $ is $\mathbb{R}$ equipped with its euclidean metric, and we set $x := 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-1186: For $(y,r) \in \mathbb{R}\times \mathbb{R _+$ we let $\Pi_{y,r}$ be the law of a one-dimensional Brownian motion and its local time at $0$ started from $(y,r)$.}
elements.sent-2411-1187: The local time is unique up to a multiplicative constant, that we fix here according to the following approximation: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_t = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \int_0^t \dd s \, 1_{\{ |\xi_s| \leq \epsilon \}}, \quad \Pi_{y,r}\text{-a.s.}\end{equation} Straightforward computations give that assumptions \ref{continuity_snake_2 , \ref{Asssumption_2 and \ref{Asssumption_3 are satisfied, which allows us to consider the measures $\mathbb{N _{y,r}$ for $(y,r) \in \mathbb{R}\times \mathbb{R _+$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1188: We write $\mathbf{N _0^*$ for the corresponding excursion measure at $0$.}
elements.sent-2411-1189: In this setting, under $\mathbb{N _{y,r , \mathbf{N _0^*$ and for every $s \geq 0$, the measure $\rho_s$ is just the Lebesgue measure restricted to $[0, H(\rho_s)]$, and $H(\rho)$ under $\mathbb{N _{y,r}$ is distributed as a non-negative Brownian excursion.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1191: Hence, the exploration process is of no use, and for this reason in \cite{ALG15 the results are stated solely in terms of $\overline{W}$ (which is now a Markov process).}
elements.sent-2411-1192: However, in order to keep the same notation and framework as in the rest of the manuscript, we will still work with the pair $(\rho,\overline{W )$.}
elements.sent-2411-1193: In \cite{ALG15 , the authors established that there exists a unique measure $\mathbb{N ^*_0$ on $\mathbb{D (\mathbb{R _+, \mathcal{M _f(\mathbb{R _+)\times \mathcal{W _{\mathbb{R )$ verifying \begin{equation}\mathbb{N}_0^*\big( \Phi(\rho, W)\big)=\lim \limits_{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \varepsilon^{-1} \mathbb{N}_{\varepsilon,0}\big( \Phi \circ \mathrm{tr}(\rho,W) \big),\end{equation} for every non-negative bounded continuous function $\Phi$ vanishing in \begin{equation}\big\{(\upvarrho,\omega)\in D(R_+, M_f(R_+)\times W_{R}):~ \sup_{s\geq 0} |\widehat{\omega}_s|<\delta\big\},\end{equation} for some $\delta>0$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1194: Then, the following measure was considered: \begin{equation}\mathbb{M}_0:=\frac{1}{2}\big(\mathbb{N}_0^* +\check{\mathbb{N}}_0^*\big),\end{equation} where $\check{\mathbb{N _0^*$ is the image of $\mathbb{N _0^*$ by the mapping $(\upvarrho,\omega)\mapsto (\upvarrho,-\omega)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1195: The measure $\mathbb{M _0$ was named the excursion measure away from $0$ of Brownian motion indexed by the Brownian tree.}
elements.sent-2411-1196: Their analysis often relies on the following master formula.
elements.sent-2411-1197: For every non-negative measurable functions $g: \mathbb{R \to \mathbb{R _+$ and $\Phi : \mathbb{D (\mathbb{R _+, \mathcal{M _f(\mathbb{R _+)\times \mathcal{W _{\mathbb{R ) \to \mathbb{R _+$, we have \begin{equation}\label{equation:averagingN+Localtime} \mathbb{N}_{0,0} \Big( \sum_{u \in \mathcal{D}} g(\widehat{\Lambda}_u) \Phi(\rho^{u,*}, W^{u,*}) \Big) = \int_0^\infty \dd \ell \, g(\ell) \mathbb{M}_{0}( \Phi ).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1198: As we shall see, \eqref{equation:averagingN+Localtime is a special case of the exit formula proved in Theorem \ref{theorem:exit and this fact will allow us to relate the measure $\mathbb{M _{0}$ with $\mathbf{N _0^*$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1200: Theorem \ref{theorem:exit entails that the left hand-side of \eqref{equation:averagingN+Localtime equals: \begin{equation}\mathbb{N}_{0,0}\Big( \int_0^\sigma \mathrm{d}A_s ~ g(\widehat{\Lambda}_s) \cdot \mathbf{N}_{0,{\widehat{\Lambda}_s}}\big(\Phi \circ \mathrm{tr}( \rho , W) \big)\Big).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-1206: A more precise version, which we now recall, was later proved in the work \cite{Disks .}
elements.sent-2411-1207: Specifically, \cite[Corollary 37]{Disks states that, if for every $(\upvarrho, \omega)\in\mathbb{D (\mathbb{R _+, \mathcal{M _f(\mathbb{R _+) \times \mathcal{W _{\mathbb{R )$, we set: \begin{equation}\widetilde{L}^*_t(\upvarrho,\omega)= \liminf_{\varepsilon \to 0} \varepsilon^{-2} \int_{0}^{t} \mathrm{d} s~ \mathbbm{1}_{ \{ |\widehat{\omega}_s| \, \leq \, \varepsilon \}},\end{equation} then, under the excursion measure $\mathbb{M _0$, this convergence holds a.e.}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1213: Thus, to complete the proof, it suffices to show that $L(\rho,W)$ and $\widetilde{L (\rho,W)$ are indistinguishable under $\mathbf{N _{0,0}$.}}
